title: Subtitle 'the voyaging mind' fails WCAG AA (~2.2:1) at 9.5px
detail: .gagos-subtitle (lines 87-94) is color rgba(180,195,225,0.42) at font-size 9.5px / weight 500. Composited over the near-black cosmos backdrop (--canvas #050507 / superbrain --bg #030108) the effective color is ~rgb(78,84,97), relative luminance ~0.085, giving a contrast ratio of ~2.2:1 against the void. WCAG 2.2 AA requires 4.5:1 for this small non-large text. The element is purely decorative-looking but is real product copy ('THE VOYAGING MIND'); it is unreadable for low-vision users and even hard for sighted users. The 0.42 alpha is the root cause; the small uppercase 9.5px size compounds it. Note the same string is the hero subtitle and ALSO appears in the JSX (.gagos-subtitle, line 476).
proposedFix: Raise the alpha so the composited color clears 4.5:1. e.g. color: rgba(180,195,225,0.62) -> ~rgb(115,124,143), L~0.19, ratio ~4.6:1. Better: rgba(190,202,226,0.7) for headroom. This is a legibility floor, not a brightness/aesthetic call — fixing the ratio does not change the hue or the design language.

verdict.reason:
Confirmed by reading GagosChrome.css:87-94 — .gagos-subtitle is color rgba(180,195,225,0.42), font-size 9.5px, font-weight 500, uppercase. The .gagos-chrome overlay (line 7-16) is transparent (pointer-events:none, no background) and the .gagos-lockup has no background, so the subtitle composites directly over the near-black cosmos (tokens.css --canvas #050507 / superbrain.css --bg #030108). The lockup is anchored top-left (top:30px/left:36px), away from the central nebula bloom, so the void is the dominant backdrop. Compositing 0.42-alpha rgb(180,195,225) over #050507 yields ~rgb(79,85,99), relative luminance ~0.091, contrast ~2.6-2.8:1 (I get ~2.75, slightly higher than the finding's ~2.2, but both are far below the 4.5:1 WCAG AA floor for small text). At 9.5px/weight 500 this is small text, so 4.5:1 applies and it fails. The string IS real product copy: JSX renders
the voyaging mind
at GagosChrome.jsx:476 — the brand identity tagline, not a placeholder. Not already handled, not a false positive. fixClass is correctly objective-safe: the proposed fix raises alpha (0.42->~0.62) on the same cool-grey hue to clear the legibility floor; it does not touch the sacred tetrad, does not change hue/design language, and is a legibility minimum, not an operator brightness/beauty call. Severity lowered high->medium: it is a small, non-interactive, decorative-position brand tagline (not body/interactive copy), and over the brighter nebula regions the ratio improves — but worst-case at its actual screen position it genuinely fails AA, so the finding stands.

title: Input placeholder 'talk to GAGOS…' fails AA (~2.1:1)
detail: .gagos-input::placeholder (lines 296-298) is rgba(190,202,226,0.4) at 15px. It sits inside .gagos-bar whose background is rgba(8,5,20,0.58) (line 271) over the near-black void, so the composited bar is ~rgb(6,4,13) and the placeholder over it composites to ~rgb(80,83,98), L~0.083, contrast ~2.1:1 vs that bar. The placeholder is the ONLY visible affordance telling a first-time user the box is a chat input (and it swaps to 'listening…' during voice), so it is essential text, not hint chrome. WCAG AA wants 4.5:1; placeholders are commonly exempted only when not relied upon — here it is relied upon. Low-vision users will not see the prompt.
proposedFix: Raise placeholder alpha to ~0.62-0.7: rgba(190,202,226,0.62) over the bar -> ~rgb(120,124,138), ratio ~4.6:1. Keeps the 'quiet' feel while clearing AA. Pure legibility fix, hue unchanged.

verdict.reason: Confirmed by reading GagosChrome.css:296-298 (.gagos-input::placeholder = rgba(190,202,226,0.4) at the 15px from .gagos-input line 293) over .gagos-bar background rgba(8,5,20,0.58) at line 271. Compositing the bar over the near-black cosmos yields ~rgb(6,4,13); the placeholder composites to ~rgb(80,83,98) (L~0.087), giving a contrast ratio of ~2.6:1 against that bar (my WCAG calc; the finding's 2.1:1 is a touch conservative but the same conclusion). That is well under the 4.5:1 AA floor for normal text, so the placeholder objectively fails AA — REAL. Two caveats that adjust severity, not validity: (1) The 'ONLY visible affordance' claim is overstated. In the zero-state, GagosChrome.jsx:501-523 renders a welcome block directly above the bar — a higher-contrast greeting 'I'm GAGOS… Where shall we begin?' (rgba(236,242,255,0.9)) plus three clickable starter prompts (rgba(228,236,250,0.84)) — that clearly signals the box is a chat input, and the input carries aria-label='Talk to GAGOS' (line 556) so screen-reader users are unaffected. So the placeholder is not the sole cue and blind users are already covered. (2) But once a turn is sent the welcome disappears (renders only when messages.length===0) and the placeholder swaps to 'listening…' during voice — in those states it IS the primary in-box hint and still fails AA, hurting low-vision sighted users. The proposed fix (raise alpha ~0.4→0.62) is alpha-only on the same rgb(190,202,226) hue: it does NOT touch the sacred poster tetrad (cyan/purple/green/orange) and is NOT a per-posture brightness/glow/density change on the being — it is utilitarian chrome text legibility. So fixClass stays objective-safe (not aesthetic-rtx). Severity lowered high→medium because the welcome greeting, starter buttons, and aria-label backstop first-time and assistive-tech users; the gap mainly bites the post-first-message and voice states for low-vision users.

title: Skip-to-chat focus ring is a dark outline on a cyan chip — low ring contrast
detail: .gagos-skip:focus (lines 40-45) sets outline: 2px solid #06121a (near-black) with the button background #7bf5fb (cyan, line 34). WCAG 2.2 SC 2.4.13 / 1.4.11 want a focus indicator with >=3:1 against ADJACENT colors. A dark ring on a bright cyan chip is actually high-contrast against the chip (good), but the ring's OUTER edge sits against the near-black page (#050507), so the dark outline is nearly invisible where it meets the page — the focus boundary on the outer side has ~1:1 contrast. The text color #06121a on #7bf5fb itself is fine (~13:1). The risk is the focus indicator can read as having no visible outer boundary against the void.
proposedFix: Use a two-tone or lighter outer ring: e.g. outline: 2px solid #06121a plus a box-shadow halo 0 0 0 4px rgba(255,255,255,0.6), OR switch the ring to a light color (outline-color: #eaf0fb) so it is visible against BOTH the cyan chip and the black page. Verify >=3:1 on both adjacencies.

verdict.reason: Confirmed by reading GagosChrome.css and the page background. Lines 33-34: skip chip is color:#06121a on background:#7bf5fb (cyan). Lines 40-45: .gagos-skip:focus sets outline: 2px solid #06121a with outline-offset: 2px. The page void behind it is #050507 (index.css line 14 --color-canvas, painted via body background-color: var(--canvas), and superbrain.css --bg:#030108). Color math: #06121a (rel. luminance ~0.0045) vs #050507 (~0.0013) gives a contrast ratio of ~1.06:1 — the dark ring's outer edge is effectively invisible against the cosmos. Because outline-offset:2px puts a transparent gap between the ring and the chip, the dark ring is actually bordered by near-black on BOTH sides (the gap inside AND the void outside), so the ring is even less visible than the finding states — the situation is worse, not a false positive. This is a real, measurable WCAG 2.2 SC 2.4.13 / SC 1.4.11 non-text/focus-contrast gap on a genuine keyboard-only affordance, not taste. The proposed remedy (light outline color #eaf0fb — already the chrome's base text color on line 15 — or a white box-shadow halo) introduces zero off-tetrad accent and touches none of the four sacred hues, so it is palette-safe and needs no aesthetic/operator judgment. fixClass objective-safe is correct (pure accessibility/contrast fix). Severity medium is defensible (real a11y boundary failure, but only reachable via Tab and the cyan chip itself stays highly visible — only the ring perimeter is weak); could arguably be low.

title: Global focus ring is cyan (--accent-400 #7bf5fb) — strong on void, but verify on cyan-tinted surfaces
detail: index.css :focus-visible (lines 81-85) uses outline 2px solid var(--accent-400) = #7bf5fb. Against the near-black cosmos this is ~12:1 (excellent). However the starter buttons (.gagos-starter, focus-visible line 424-427) and retry (line 161) put a cyan ring (rgba(123,245,251,0.6)) on a cyan-tinted hover/border surface (border rgba(123,245,251,0.12-0.34), hover bg rgba(123,245,251,0.07)). On the brightest cyan-tinted state the cyan ring's contrast against the adjacent cyan-ish fill can dip toward the 3:1 SC 1.4.11 floor. Most states pass; the worst-case hovered+focused starter is the one to verify.
proposedFix: Either raise the starter/retry focus ring alpha to a solid rgba(123,245,251,0.9)+ and add offset (already 2px), or pair it with a thin dark inner stroke (box-shadow inset 0 0 0 1px rgba(3,1,12,0.8)) so the ring is delimited on cyan fills. Low priority; on the default near-black backdrop the ring is fine.

title: Starter eyebrow 'the voyaging mind · listening' is borderline (~6:1) and the arrow is decorative-only (handled)
detail: .gagos-welcome__eyebrow (lines 371-377) rgba(123,245,251,0.6) over void composites to ~rgb(76,149,152), L~0.26, ratio ~6.0:1 — PASSES AA for its 10px uppercase bold, but it is the thinnest passing element and would fail if the alpha drops. Separately, the starter arrow '→' (.gagos-starter__arrow, line 439-444, and JSX line 518 marked aria-hidden) is rgba(123,245,251,0.5) ~5:1 and correctly aria-hidden, and the icon glyph (.gagos-starter__icon #7bf5fb) is decorative beside a full text label — so no color-only signaling problem here. Recording the eyebrow as a watch-item, not a failure.
proposedFix: No change required for AA. If hardening: bump eyebrow to rgba(123,245,251,0.72) (~7:1) for AAA headroom. Leave the arrow aria-hidden as-is.

title: No color-only signaling in pills — state, model, supervised, offline all carry a TEXT label (verified good)
detail: Audited the 'STATUS FROM BODY' concern for color-only meaning. Every status pill pairs its hue dot with a textual label: model pill -> modelLine text + purple dot (JSX 480-483); state pill -> PHASE_META[convPhase].label ('resting/thinking/streaming/complete/error') + colored dot (JSX 484-493, PHASE_META 40-47); supervised pill -> word 'supervised' + green dot (494-497); offline -> the word 'offline' + red dot (481-482). The dot colors (#b06eff purple, #54f0a0 green, #f87171 red, plus PHASE_META #22d3ee/#34d399/#a855f7) are redundant encodings, not the sole signal. There is ALSO a screen-reader live region (.gagos-sr-only role=status aria-live=polite, JSX 471) narrating 'GAGOS is thinking/replying/offline'. This is a correctly-built non-color-dependent status system — no fix needed.
proposedFix: No change. Keep the text label + aria-live pairing. If anything, ensure PHASE_META labels never get hidden in a future 'dot-only' compact mode without an aria-label.

title: Mic listening red rgba(255,90,120) / #ff9bb0 is OFF the sacred tetrad (poster has no pink-red)
detail: The sacred accent palette is cyan #7bf5fb, purple #b06eff, green #54f0a0, orange #ff7e40 — the poster's error/warm channel is ORANGE #ff7e40, and the offline dot in this same file uses #f87171 (a coral-red). The mic listening state instead introduces rgba(255,90,120) / #ff9bb0 (a pink-magenta red) at lines 322-327 and 352-354. That is a third distinct red/pink not in the tetrad and not matching either the orange error channel or the #f87171 offline red. This is a DEVIATION flag (not a request to recolor for taste): per palette law I flag off-tetrad accents. Whether the 'listening' affordance should map to the orange channel (#ff7e40) or stay a distinct alert red is a design-bible decision, but the current pink #ff9bb0 matches nothing in the canon.
proposedFix: Decide the canonical hue for the listening/recording state: either reuse the offline/error red #f87171 already in this file (consistency) or the poster error-orange #ff7e40. Replace rgba(255,90,120)/#ff9bb0 accordingly. This is a palette-canon alignment, not an intensity/RTX change.

verdict.reason: CONFIRMED by reading GagosChrome.css. The mic listening state at lines 322-327 uses background rgba(255,90,120,0.18), color #ff9bb0, and shadow rgba(255,120,150,...)/rgba(255,90,120,...); the gagos-pulse keyframes at lines 352-354 repeat rgba(255,120,150,...) and rgba(255,90,120,...). These are pink-magenta reds (~#ff5a78 / #ff7896 / #ff9bb0, hue ~345-350 deg) that match NOTHING in the sacred tetrad (cyan #7bf5fb, purple #b06eff, green #54f0a0, orange #ff7e40). The poster's warm/error channel is orange #ff7e40 (~18 deg), and this same file's offline dot (lines 141-144) uses a separate coral-red #f87171. So three distinct warm reds now coexist, and the mic pink is the furthest off-canon. This is a genuine palette DEVIATION the law asks agents to flag, NOT a request to recolor for taste and NOT an intensity/RTX change (the finding explicitly avoids asking to brighten/densify). It was missed by the recent poster-palette alignment commit cba574f, which aligned the dots but not the mic state, so it is not already handled. It is not a misread: the cited colors are present verbatim at the cited lines. fixClass stays needs-decision: the deviation is objective, but its resolution requires a design-bible choice — a 'listening/recording' affordance is conventionally red (collapsing it to the orange error channel could be semantically wrong), so the operator must decide between reusing the file's #f87171 for internal consistency, adopting poster orange #ff7e40, or sanctioning a distinct alert hue; there is no single mechanically-correct mapping, which keeps it out of objective-safe. Severity medium is fair: a visible interactive control off-canon, but small and transient.

title: Rogue blue #60a5fa / rgba(59,130,246) survives in --border-accent, --elevation-4, --glow-accent (poster has zero blue)
detail: tokens.css still carries blue: --border-accent: rgba(96,165,250,0.35) (line 22, #60a5fa), --elevation-4 ends in 0 0 0 1px rgba(59,130,246,0.06) (line 66), --glow-accent: 0 0 20px rgba(59,130,246,0.18) (line 67). The accent ramp itself was migrated to cyan/purple (lines 35-45) per the poster, and the @theme accent is #7bf5fb, but these three blue residues remain. The poster has ZERO blue; any blue glow/border on a focused or elevated surface is an off-tetrad deviation. Accessibility-adjacent because --glow-accent / --elevation-4 can tint focus/elevation rings, and --border-accent could be used as a focus border elsewhere, subtly desaturating the cyan canon.
proposedFix: Retune the three blue residues to the cyan anchor: --border-accent: rgba(123,245,251,0.35); --elevation-4 ring -> rgba(123,245,251,0.06); --glow-accent: 0 0 20px rgba(123,245,251,0.18). Hue-only swap to the sacred cyan, no intensity change.

verdict.reason: CONFIRMED by reading frontend/src/styles/tokens.css. All three cited blue residues are present at the exact lines: L22 --border-accent: rgba(96,165,250,0.35) (= #60a5fa, Tailwind blue-400); L66 --elevation-4 ends in `0 0 0 1px rgba(59,130,246,0.06)` (= #3b82f6, blue-500); L67 --glow-accent: 0 0 20px rgba(59,130,246,0.18) (blue-500). These are genuine off-tetrad blues, and the VARIANT-H poster has zero blue. The file's own comment (L31-34) declares the law ("poster has zero blue... Anchor is the poster cyan #7bf5fb"), and the accent ramp (L35-45) plus --info (L54) were already migrated to cyan/purple — so these three are real leftover residues that escaped the migration, not already-handled and not a misread. The proposed fix is a pure hue-only swap to the sacred cyan (rgba(123,245,251,...)) with no intensity change, so it is correctly objective-safe and preserves the RTX intensity that belongs to the operator. CORRECTION to severity: medium is overstated. Grep across the entire frontend/ tree shows --border-accent, --elevation-4, and --glow-accent are defined ONLY in tokens.css and consumed by ZERO files. They are dead/unconsumed tokens, so the blue currently tints no surface, focus ring, or border — the "accessibility-adjacent / could tint focus rings" argument is speculative ("could be used elsewhere"), not a live deviation. This is token-consistency / dead-code hygiene that future-proofs against the residue being wired up later, hence severity lowered medium->low. fixClass confirmed objective-safe.

title: Assistant bubble body text passes, but verify over the brightest brain particles (worst-case over cyan glass is fine)
detail: .gagos-msg--gagos (lines 216-226) text rgba(238,244,255,0.93) on bg rgba(123,245,251,0.05) over void -> composited text ~rgb(222,228,238), L~0.74, ratio ~15:1 over the near-black void: PASS (AAA). User bubble .gagos-msg--user (208-215) #eef0ff on rgba(176,110,255,0.14) over void -> ~16:1, PASS. The only theoretical risk is the bottom-left chat sitting over a bright lobe of the particle brain; but the being is centered/right per the layout comments and the bubbles have a dark-ish translucent fill + text-shadow (line 224) that protect legibility. Recording as verified-pass for completeness.
proposedFix: No change required. The text-shadow on .gagos-msg--gagos (0 1px 12px rgba(0,0,0,0.5)) already hardens it against bright backdrops; keep it.

detail:
The GAGOS wordmark (line 474 `

The 3D hero is aria-hidden (WorkspaceCanvas.tsx line 214 `.scene-layer aria-hidden="true"`), so the DOM chrome is the ONLY thing a screen-reader user perceives — and it has no programmatic title or section heading. Screen-reader users navigating by heading (a primary navigation mode) land on a page with nothing. The product identity 'GAGOS' is purely visual.
proposedFix: Make the wordmark the document's h1 (e.g. wrap 'GAGOS' in <h1 className="gagos-wordmark"> with the visual styling preserved), and promote the greeting to an h2 or add an sr-only <h2> like 'Conversation with GAGOS' above the thread so heading navigation reaches the chat. Keep visual appearance identical via CSS.

title: Interactive chat lives outside any landmark; the only <main> wraps the aria-hidden canvas
detail: SuperbrainApp renders <WorkspaceCanvas/> and <GagosChrome/> as siblings (lines 20-23). The single <main className="superbrain-experience"> is inside WorkspaceCanvas (WorkspaceCanvas.tsx line 213) and contains ONLY the aria-hidden `.scene-layer`. GagosChrome's root is a plain <div className="gagos-chrome"> (GagosChrome.jsx line 467) with no role. Result: the entire usable interface (status, chat thread, input, controls) is orphaned content not inside any landmark, while the one <main> landmark exposes nothing to AT. Landmark navigation (rotor / 'skip to main') is broken — there's effectively no main content region.
proposedFix: Move the <main> landmark (or add role="main") to wrap the GagosChrome chrome (the actual content), and demote the aria-hidden canvas wrapper to a plain decorative <div>. At minimum give `.gagos-chrome` role="main" with an aria-label like 'GAGOS conversation'. Avoid two <main>s — there must be exactly one.

verdict.reason: Confirmed true by reading all three cited files. SuperbrainApp.jsx lines 20-23: <WorkspaceCanvas/> and <GagosChrome/> are siblings inside a plain <div className="font-sans antialiased"> (not a landmark). WorkspaceCanvas.tsx line 213 opens <main className="superbrain-experience"> whose only child is <div className="scene-layer" aria-hidden="true"> (line 214, closes at 250) — so the single <main> landmark exposes nothing to AT. GagosChrome.jsx line 467 root is <div className="gagos-chrome"> with NO role; a grep for <main|role="main"|<header|<nav|<section returned zero matches, while role="status" (471) and role="group" (502) are the only roles. The actual interactive UI lives entirely in GagosChrome: chat <input> (546), mic/send buttons (559/569), starter-prompt and retry buttons — all orphaned outside any landmark. So the only <main> is a decoy wrapping aria-hidden canvas, and the usable interface sits in no landmark; rotor/skip-to-main navigation is effectively broken. Mitigating fact: the input is still reachable via the 'Skip to the chat' button (468) and normal tab order, so the UI is operable — hence high, not critical. fixClass objective-safe is correct: adding role=\"main\"+aria-label to .gagos-chrome and demoting the canvas <main> to a plain decorative <div> (keeping exactly one main) is a pure landmark-correctness fix with no visual/palette/intensity impact, so it touches nothing in the sacred-tetrad or RTX-fidelity domain. Caveat for the fixer: WorkspaceCanvas.tsx is lab-mirrored (GAG demo/gag-orchestrator), so the <main>->div change must also be applied to the lab copy; GagosChrome is product-only.

title: Chat thread has no log role and messages don't convey author to AT
detail: The thread container (line 524 `<div className="gagos-thread" ref={threadRef}>`) has no role="log"/aria-label, and each message bubble (line 528 `<div className="gagos-msg gagos-msg--${m.role}">`) distinguishes user vs GAGOS purely by CSS color/alignment (GagosChrome.css lines 208-226). A screen-reader user hears a flat run of text with no indication of who said what, and incoming GAGOS replies are not announced as they arrive (the thread is not a live region). The separate sr-only region (line 471) only narrates coarse phase state ('GAGOS is thinking/replying'), not the actual message content, so the conversation's substance is inaccessible.
proposedFix: Add role="log" aria-label="Conversation" aria-live="polite" (or a dedicated polite live region mirroring new GAGOS text) to the thread, and give each message an sr-only author prefix or aria-label, e.g. prepend an sr-only span 'You:' / 'GAGOS:' per bubble keyed on m.role. This makes turn authorship and incoming replies perceivable without sight.

verdict.reason: Confirmed by reading GagosChrome.jsx and GagosChrome.css. (1) The thread container at line 524 `<div className="gagos-thread" ref={threadRef}>` has no role="log", no aria-label, and no aria-live — verified, it is a bare div. (2) Each message at line 528 `<div key={m.id} className={`gagos-msg gagos-msg--${m.role}`}>` carries no role, aria-label, or author text; CSS lines 208-226 confirm user vs GAGOS is conveyed PURELY by align-self (flex-end vs flex-start) and color tint (purple rgba(176,110,255) vs cyan rgba(123,245,251)) — no ::before author content exists anywhere. So a screen-reader user hears a flat, unattributed run of text. (3) The sr-only region at line 471 with role="status" aria-live="polite" only emits statusAnnouncement (lines 454-464): "GAGOS is thinking/replying/replied/offline" — coarse phase, never the reply substance. Streamed reply text lands via updateMessage() into the non-live thread div, so incoming GAGOS content is never announced. This is genuinely unhandled (no aria-live/role/author prefix anywhere in the file), not a misread (every line number and CSS rule matches), and not taste — it is WCAG 1.3.1 (Info & Relationships) and 4.1.3 (Status Messages). The proposed fix (role="log" aria-label, plus per-bubble sr-only "You:"/"GAGOS:" prefix keyed on m.role, or a polite live region mirroring new GAGOS text) is purely additive markup with zero visual/palette/density impact, so fixClass objective-safe is correct. Severity lowered high→medium: the primary input is already accessible (input aria-label, skip link) and a coarse status IS announced, so the substance/authorship gap is a real degradation but not a total access lockout.

title: No Escape handling — cannot clear the draft or stop active listening from the keyboard
detail: The input onKeyDown (lines 553-555) handles only Enter. There is no Escape branch. A keyboard user who has typed a draft has no quick clear, and — more importantly — when voice capture is active (listening, lines 408-409), the only stop affordance is clicking the mic; Escape does not stop recognition. This is a standard expectation for a text/voice input and its absence is a keyboard-operability gap.
proposedFix: Extend the input onKeyDown: on e.key === 'Escape', if listening call recognitionRef.current?.stop(); else if draft is non-empty, setDraft(''). Optionally blur on a second Escape. Keep Enter behavior unchanged.

verdict.reason: Verified by reading GagosChrome.jsx. The input onKeyDown (lines 553-555) has exactly one branch: `if (e.key === 'Enter') { e.preventDefault(); void submit(draft); }`. A file-wide search for Escape/keydown/addEventListener confirms there is NO Escape branch and NO document-level keyboard listener anywhere. So (a) a keyboard user cannot clear a typed draft with Escape, and (b) while voice capture is active (`listening` set in rec.onstart at line 408), there is no keyboard path to stop recognition via Escape — toggleMic (429-439) is bound to onClick only. The proposed fix (Escape -> if listening recognitionRef.current?.stop(); else if draft non-empty setDraft('')) is sound and matches the existing code shape. This is a genuine, conventional keyboard-operability gap, not a false positive, misread, or already-handled case. One correction to the finding's framing: the mic IS a real <button> with aria-label={listening ? 'Stop listening' : ...} (lines 559-567), so it is keyboard-focusable — a user CAN stop listening by tabbing to the mic and pressing Enter/Space. There is no hard keyboard trap; missing Escape is a standard-convention convenience gap, not the ONLY keyboard affordance. fixClass stays objective-safe (a conventional accessibility affordance, no brightness/density/palette/RTX or operator-choice judgment; sacred-palette and aesthetic-rtx laws do not apply). Severity downgraded medium->low because the stop-listening action is already keyboard-reachable via the focusable mic button, so impact is convenience, not a blocker.

title: Retry button lacks accessible context (which message / what is being retried)
detail: On a failed turn a 'Retry' button is appended inside the error bubble (lines 534-538). Its only accessible name is the visible text 'Retry'. A screen-reader user navigating by button (or who lands on it out of context) hears just 'Retry' with no indication of what failed or what will be re-sent. The error message text is a sibling, not programmatically associated.

title: Camera autoRotate spins continuously with no prefers-reduced-motion guard (the #1 vestibular trigger)

Fixer note: this file is lab-mirrored, so the same change must also land in 'GAG demo/gag-orchestrator/src/components/canvas/CosmicBackground.tsx'.

title: Canvas reduced-motion is captured ONCE and never reacts to a runtime OS toggle
detail: Every canvas/scene reduced-motion read is one-shot, non-reactive: SuperbrainScene `reducedMotionRef = useRef(shouldReduceMotion())` (line 1193), BrainModel `reduceMotion = useMemo(() => shouldReduceMotion(), [])` (line 722), MaterializationLayer `useMemo(() => shouldReduceMotion(), [])` (line 722 of that file), BrainPointField `reduce = useMemo(...matchMedia..., [])` (lines 168-174), BootSequence `useState(() => ...matchMedia...)` (line 110). None register a matchMedia 'change' listener. shouldReduceMotion in lib/openingMotion.ts (lines 87-90) only reads `.matches` once. Contrast the HUD, which uses the reactive `useReducedMotion()` from motion/react, and CyberCursor, which registers a matchMedia change listener for `(pointer: fine)`. Net effect: a user who toggles 'reduce motion' at the OS while the app is open gets no change in the entire 3D layer until a full reload — the most motion-heavy part of the product is the least responsive to the preference.
proposedFix: Promote shouldReduceMotion to a small reactive hook (subscribe to matchMedia('(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)').addEventListener('change', ...) via useSyncExternalStore, mirroring CyberCursor's pattern) and thread its value down (it is already passed as props to NodeLattice/MaterializationLayer/NervousSystem). This also lets a single source feed the autoRotate/Float/starfield fixes above.

verdict.reason: VERIFIED REAL by reading every cited file. The entire 3D/canvas layer reads prefers-reduced-motion ONCE at mount with no matchMedia 'change' listener: SuperbrainScene.tsx:1193 `useRef(shouldReduceMotion())` (comment literally says "captured once"); SuperbrainScene.tsx:722 BrainModel `useMemo(() => shouldReduceMotion(), [])` (empty deps); BrainPointField.tsx:168-174 `useMemo(...matchMedia(...).matches, [])`; BootSequence.tsx:110-112 `useState(() => ...matchMedia(...).matches)`; openingMotion.ts:87-90 `shouldReduceMotion` reads `.matches` once with no subscription. None register a change listener. The contrast holds: HUD uses the REACTIVE `useReducedMotion()` from motion/react (SuperbrainHUD.tsx:5,283,319,599) and its own comment (lines 268-272) documents that the synchronous matchMedia helper was REMOVED precisely because "a runtime toggle would not re-evaluate" — yet that fix was never propagated to the 3D layer; CyberCursor uses useSyncExternalStore + addEventListener('change') (lines 47-58), the exact pattern the proposed fix cites. Net effect as described: a mid-session OS reduced-motion toggle does not affect the most motion-heavy surface until a full reload. Two inaccuracies in the finding, both cosmetic and non-load-bearing: (1) MaterializationLayer does NOT have a local `useMemo(() => shouldReduceMotion(), [])` at line 722 — it RECEIVES `reducedMotion` as a PROP (MaterializationLayer.tsx:96) fed from SuperbrainScene's one-shot `reduceMotion` (SuperbrainScene.tsx:947), so it is still non-reactive, just by inheritance; (2) the finding missed NervousSystem.tsx:441, which reads matchMedia per-frame inside useFrame and is therefore the lone effectively-reactive (but ad-hoc) instance. Neither changes the conclusion. fixClass is correctly objective-safe: it is a WCAG 2.3.3 accessibility plumbing/consistency fix (subscribe to matchMedia 'change' via a reactive hook mirroring CyberCursor) that only changes how an already-existing boolean is delivered — it touches NO palette, brightness, density, particle count, or intensity, so it is not an aesthetic-rtx operator call. The reactive pattern already exists twice in-repo, making this a consistency fix, not new design. Severity medium is right: real vestibular-accessibility gap on the heaviest motion surface, but only manifests on a mid-session OS toggle without reload (preference IS honored on initial load and any reload).

area: scene / sky dome (mesh mode + parallax pattern)
title: Sky dome runs pointer-parallax + nebula drift + star twinkle with no reduced-motion guard
detail: KnowledgeHorizon's useFrame (lines 265-284) feeds uTime (drives nebula drift `drift = uTime*0.006` line 170 and per-star `twinkle` line 122) and an eased pointer (lines 274-275) into a three-layer differential parallax (uvFar/uvMid/uvNear, lines 156-160) that shifts the entire sky as the pointer moves. There is no reduced-motion handling. NOTE: in the current official points being this is gated off (line 1617 requires BEING_MODE !== 'points'), so it does not ship in the default boot today — but it is live in mesh mode and is the canonical 'parallax that should be suppressed under reduced-motion' pattern, so it should be fixed alongside the others if mesh mode is ever surfaced.
proposedFix: Pass a reducedMotion prop; when set, stop advancing uTime (freeze nebula drift + twinkle) and pin uPointer to (0,0) so the parallax layers don't track the pointer. The static sky remains fully rendered.
locator: useFrame 265-284; drift line 170; twinkle line 122; parallax 156-160; mount gate SuperbrainScene.tsx:1617

verdict.reason: Verified against the cited files. KnowledgeHorizon.tsx has no reduced-motion handling: its signature is `KnowledgeHorizon({ activity }: { activity: number })` — no reducedMotion prop, and no shouldReduceMotion / prefers-reduced-motion reference anywhere in the file. Every line claim checks out exactly: useFrame at 265-284 unconditionally advances uTime (line 278) and eases+copies the pointer (274-282); drift = uTime*0.006 at line 170; per-star twinkle sin(uTime...) at line 122; three-layer differential pointer parallax uvFar/uvMid/uvNear at 158-160 (par = uPointer*0.08 at 157). So the entire sky drifts (nebula), twinkles (stars), and tracks the pointer with zero reduced-motion guard. The mount gate is confirmed verbatim at SuperbrainScene.tsx:1617 — `sky === 'layered' && tier !== 'low' && BEING_MODE !== 'points'` — so this path is mesh-only and does NOT ship in the default points-being boot today; the finding states this honestly. The proposed fix (pass reducedMotion, freeze uTime + pin uPointer to 0,0, keep static sky fully rendered) is deterministic, palette-neutral, and exactly mirrors the established sibling pattern — NodeLattice, PointerBrainClone, NervousSystem, and MaterializationLayer in the same scene all already take a reducedMotion/reduceMotion prop. This is a token/pattern-consistency + vestibular-accessibility gap with no aesthetic judgment, so objective-safe is correct. Severity lowered medium -> low because the code path is gated off in the single official frontend and reaches no user today (only relevant if mesh mode is ever surfaced).

title: Skill-star galaxy orbits + twinkles continuously with no reduced-motion guard
detail: MemoryGalaxy's useFrame (lines 183-186) advances uTime, which the vertex shader uses to orbit every star (`angle = aAngle + uTime * aSpeed`, line 49) and the fragment shader uses to twinkle/pulse (lines 81-83). No reduced-motion handling exists. Mitigations: orbital periods are deliberately minutes-long (aSpeed ~0.008-0.014, line 144) and the layer is dormant until real pheromone trails exist (line 188), AND it is gated off in the points being (line 1632, SHOW_MEMORY_GALAXY && BEING_MODE !== 'points'). So motion is slow and currently not in the default boot — hence low severity — but it still ignores the preference where it does render.

locator: useFrame 183-186; orbit shader line 49; twinkle/pulse 81-83; mount gate SuperbrainScene.tsx:1632

title: Orbiting synaptic sparks + flicker animate every frame with no reduced-motion guard
detail: NeuralAura's sparks orbit on the shared uTime leaf (vertex shader `angle = aPhase + uTime * aSpeed`, line 202; flicker line 212) and the useFrame (lines 349-372) damps color/activity each frame. NeuralAura takes no reducedMotion prop and has no matchMedia read. It is mounted only in mesh mode (it is inside the BrainModel mesh path, not the points being), so it does not ship in the default boot today — low severity — but the orbiting motes ignore the preference where they render.
proposedFix: Thread the reactive reduced-motion value in and, when set, stop advancing the shared time the sparks read (or pin spark uTime) so the motes hold still; the shells (fresnel volume) are static already.
locator: useFrame 349-372; orbit shader line 202; flicker line 212

title: Phase-7 reabsorption motes always play the full rise animation, ignoring reduced-motion
detail: ReabsorptionParticles animates ~72 motes streaming up the spine over durationMs (default 1300ms) in its useFrame (lines 55-79). It accepts no reducedMotion prop, and MaterializationLayer renders it (lines 434-437) without passing one — every other child there (MaterializedTab/AnatomicalConductorOverlay/AttentionConductionPulse/CompletionMemoryBead) receives reducedMotion, so this is an inconsistency. It runs in the live points being during a tab reabsorb. Severity is low because it is transient (one-shot, ~1.3s, event-driven, modest local translation) rather than a continuous vestibular loop — but for full coverage a reduced-motion user should get the slab snapping to absorbed without the mote travel.
proposedFix: Add a `reducedMotion` prop (passed from MaterializationLayer's existing `reducedMotion`); when set, render the end state immediately (or skip the mote travel and jump opacity to 0) so the tab disappears without the up-the-spine motion. Keeps the phase-7 outcome, drops the motion.
locator: ReabsorptionParticles useFrame 55-79 (no prop); MaterializationLayer.tsx render 434-437 (reducedMotion not passed)

area: nervous system (mesh mode) — perf note alongside motion
title: Reduced-motion is correctly honored but re-queried via matchMedia every single frame
detail: NervousSystem DOES honor reduced-motion (it freezes the flow advance, lines 448-457) which is good. But it calls `window.matchMedia('(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)').matches` inside the useFrame on EVERY frame (lines 439-444) to refresh NERVE_REDUCEMOTION_UNIFORM. matchMedia-per-frame is wasteful (a synchronous platform query at 60fps) and is the wrong way to get reactivity — a change listener is the correct pattern. This is the one place that IS reactive, but via the costliest possible mechanism. (Mesh-mode only; gated off in the points being at SuperbrainScene.tsx:1666.)
proposedFix: Replace the per-frame matchMedia with the shared reactive reduced-motion source (the hook proposed above) and write NERVE_REDUCEMOTION_UNIFORM from a change listener / prop, not inside useFrame. NervousSystem already receives a `reducedMotion` prop (line 339) — use it instead of re-querying.
locator: useFrame matchMedia 439-444; existing reducedMotion prop line 339

title: Shared uTime leaf advances unconditionally, so every shader's time-driven motion keeps running under reduced-motion unless each shader self-gates
detail: The scene's main useFrame sets `uniforms.uTime.value = time` every frame (line 1402) with no reduced-motion branch. uTime is THE shared clock consumed by the point-field cortex breathe/flow, NeuralAura sparks, KnowledgeHorizon drift/twinkle, and the nerve flow band. This means reduced-motion compliance is delegated piecemeal to each consumer: BrainPointField gates its posture uniforms (good), NervousSystem freezes its flow advance (good), but the sparks/sky/galaxy do NOT (see findings above). The architecture makes it easy to ship motion that ignores the preference because the master clock never stops. Note the point-cloud's in-shader breathe is driven off uTime indirectly via uFlowSpeed which IS gated, so the cloud is OK — but the pattern is fragile.
proposedFix: Decision needed: either (a) keep uTime advancing and require every time-driven shader to multiply its motion term by (1 - uReducedMotion) — then add that uniform to the sparks/sky/galaxy shaders; or (b) advance uTime at a frozen/near-zero rate under reduced-motion at the source (line 1402), accepting that any shader using uTime for non-motion (e.g. static hue) must be checked first. Option (a) is safer for fidelity (no risk of freezing a non-motion use of the clock). This is the coordinating fix that makes the per-component gaps above closeable cleanly.

verdict.reason: VERIFIED TRUE by reading the cited files. SuperbrainScene.tsx line 1402 sets `uniforms.uTime.value = time;` unconditionally inside useFrame, with no reduced-motion branch (the surrounding code DOES gate other uniforms via reducedMotionRef.current at lines 1359/1370/1411/1428/1504, so the omission for uTime is real, not a misread). The shared uTime leaf is consumed without reduced-motion gating by the named layers: NeuralAura sparks read `uTime: uniforms.uTime` (line 267) and drive `angle = aPhase + uTime * aSpeed` (line 202) plus flicker (line 212) — the file has ZERO reduced-motion references (grep: no matches). KnowledgeHorizon drives `drift = uTime * 0.006` (line 170) with no reduced-motion (grep: no matches). CosmicBackground does `transformed.z += uTime * speed` (line 176) with no reduced-motion (grep: no matches). MemoryGalaxy spins `angle = aAngle + uTime * aSpeed` + twinkle (lines 49/81/83) with no reduced-motion. The finding's two 'good' counter-examples also check out: BrainPointField gates via `if (reduce)` snapping uFlowSpeed/posture (lines 217-223), and NervousSystem freezes its flow advance under reduced motion (`if (NERVE_REDUCEMOTION_UNIFORM.value < 0.5)`, lines 448-457). So the asymmetry is exactly as described: gating is delegated piecemeal and the master clock never stops, so sparks/sky/galaxy keep animating under prefers-reduced-motion. One minor inaccuracy (does not weaken it): only NeuralAura's sparks literally consume the shared `uniforms.uTime`; KnowledgeHorizon/MemoryGalaxy/CosmicBackground advance their OWN `state.clock.elapsedTime` leaves — but those also never freeze under reduced motion, so the user-facing defect and the 'fragile architecture' diagnosis still hold. Consistent with sibling audit notes (obs 2988/3001) flagging vestibular/motion gaps across these layers.

title: Boot is autoplay but is correctly shortened, skippable, and degrades well under reduced-motion (no fix needed — documented as verified-clean)
detail: The boot sequence autoplays on every load (terminal log typing + progress bar + finale). title: Tailwind-blue residue lingers in the design-token single source of truth (--border-accent, --glow-accent, --elevation-4)
detail: tokens.css is declared the single source of truth for the design language, yet three accent tokens still carry literal Tailwind blue, which the VARIANT-H poster bans entirely (zero blue). L22 `--border-accent: rgba(96, 165, 250, 0.35)` is Tailwind blue-400; L66 `--elevation-4: ... 0 0 0 1px rgba(59,130,246,0.06)` and L67 `--glow-accent: 0 0 20px rgba(59,130,246,0.18)` are Tailwind blue-500. The accent ramp directly above them (L35-45) was correctly migrated to poster cyan #7bf5fb -> purple #b06eff, so these three are leftover blue the migration missed. Grep confirms none of the three are currently consumed anywhere in src/ (dead), but as named tokens in the canonical palette file they are exactly the off-tetrad values that get re-wired into a component later and silently reintroduce blue.

This is a hue-only swap (blue->cyan), not an intensity change.

title: constants.ts COLORS/LIGHTS define an entire OLD blue/indigo/violet palette (#6366f1 indigo, #7c3aed violet, #06b6d4 cyan, #5e8dff/#8fa8ff blue lights) that conflicts with the poster tetrad

title: Scene light rig is tinted blue / blue-violet (#8fa8ff, #bcd0ff, #795cff, #5e8dff, #c8a8ff) — off-tetrad blues with zero poster basis
detail: The lighting rig at L1642-1648 uses blue and blue-violet light colors: directionalLights #8fa8ff (periwinkle blue), #bcd0ff (pale blue), #795cff (blue-violet); pointLights #5e8dff (cornflower blue), #c8a8ff (lavender), #ff5c9a (pink). The poster has zero blue, and #8fa8ff/#bcd0ff/#5e8dff are unambiguously blue. The inline comment (L1639) states the cortex shader is self-lit and ignores these lights, and every consumer of this rig (AccretionCore L1653-1655, PointerBrainClone L1658-1660, mesh NervousSystem L1666-1668) is gated behind BEING_MODE !== 'points' — and points IS the official shipping default — so in the default build these blue lights illuminate nothing visible. They are therefore off-tetrad but currently dormant. The ambientLight #241145 (deep indigo, L1642) is the only one that could leak; it is very dark so impact is low.
locator: SuperbrainScene.tsx L1642-1648 (light rig); consumers gated at L1653, L1658, L1666
proposedFix: Because these light colors only matter in mesh mode (non-default), changing them shifts mesh-mode lighting intensity/tone, which is the operator's RTX/FIDELITY look — treat the warmth/brightness as aesthetic. The OBJECTIVE-safe part is the BLUE hue itself: nudge #8fa8ff/#bcd0ff/#5e8dff/#795cff off pure blue toward the poster's cyan/purple axis (e.g. cool key -> #7bf5fb-ward, fill -> #b06eff-ward) while preserving each light's intensity. Confirm with operator at :5173 in mesh mode before changing, since tone is a fidelity call.

verdict.reason:
CONFIRMED REAL by reading the cited rig at SuperbrainScene.tsx L1641-1648: the light colors are off-tetrad blues/blue-violets (#8fa8ff periwinkle, #bcd0ff pale blue, #795cff blue-violet, #5e8dff cornflower, plus #c8a8ff lavender and #ff5c9a pink), and the VARIANT-H poster (verified) has ZERO blue — the sacred tetrad is cyan #7bf5fb / purple #b06eff / green #54f0a0 / orange #ff7e40. So the deviation is genuine.

HOWEVER the finding's central premise is WRONG and UNDERSTATES the impact. It claims that because every consumer (AccretionCore L1653, PointerBrainClone L1658, NervousSystem L1666) is gated behind BEING_MODE !== 'points', and points is the shipping default (confirmed in beingMode.ts: default 'points'), "these blue lights illuminate nothing visible" / are "currently dormant." That is false. I traced a lights-aware consumer that IS active in points mode: MaterializedTab (the work slab, poster phases 4-6) uses meshStandardMaterial (MaterializedTab.tsx L1834-1842 slab body, L1679 connector tube, L305 buttons), and it is rendered UNGATED in MaterializationLayer.tsx L413-427 (only the mesh-era conductor overlays at L402-408 are gated by !POINTS, NOT the tab itself). The slab body material is matte/dielectric (organMaterialState.ts L236-237: roughness ~0.56-0.74, metalness ~0.05-0.12) with low emissiveIntensity 0.08 and opacity 0.92 — i.e. its look is dominated by LIT diffuse, not emission. The three blue directionals and the cornflower-blue pointLight therefore tint the central work surface — the poster's hero "Working + Showing Work" slab — with off-tetrad blue in the DEFAULT shipping build. BrainPointField itself is self-lit (raw ShaderMaterial, BrainPointField.tsx L84) and unaffected, so the brain isn't tinted, but the slab is.

CONSEQUENCES: severity should rise from medium to high (it tints a visible central element, not a dormant mesh-only path). fixClass should move from needs-decision to OBJECTIVE-SAFE for the hue: the blue→cyan/purple nudge does not require operator mesh-mode sign-off (the finding's gating rationale evaporates once the lights are shown to be visible in the default build), and blue is unambiguously off the zero-blue poster palette. The only AESTHETIC-RTX part is each light's intensity/warmth/brightness (the operator RTX call) — preserve those scalars when correcting the hue. NOTE for any fix: SuperbrainScene is lab-mirrored to GAG demo/gag-orchestrator/src/components/canvas/SuperbrainScene.tsx, so a real fix must touch both copies.

title: Point-field vertex-color fallback is blue-violet (0.6, 0.5, 0.95 = #9980f2) — leans blue if the brain GLB ever lacks vertex colors
detail: When a sampled mesh has no `color` vertex attribute, the sampler falls back to (r=0.6, g=0.5, b=0.95) = #9980f2 (L133-135), a periwinkle blue-violet weighted toward blue (b channel highest). This is the LIVE default point-being's fallback color. In practice the brain GLB carries vertex colors so the fallback is rarely hit, but if it triggers it paints the organism blue-violet, which the poster bans. The poster's organic brain palette is multi-hued (red/orange/green/lime/cyan/violet/magenta per anatomy) but never pure blue.
proposedFix: Shift the fallback off blue toward the poster's lavender 'rest' posture (e.g. r=0.62, g=0.47, b=0.96 -> use bodyPosture rest [158,120,245]/255 = 0.62,0.47,0.96, or pull b down so it is not the dominant channel). Hue-only correction; does not change point count/brightness.

title: Two parallel design-token systems define overlapping vars with DIFFERENT values (--text-1, --hairline, --success/--state-ok, accent)
detail: Three CSS layers each redeclare core tokens with conflicting values, and which one wins depends on import/cascade order rather than a single source of truth. tokens.css sets --text-1: #f2f3f7 (opaque hex), --hairline: rgba(255,255,255,0.08), --success: #34d399, --accent: var(--accent-500)=#3ad9e6. superbrain.css (loaded via index.css chain) re-sets --text-1: rgba(232,238,250,0.93), --hairline: rgba(255,255,255,0.09), --accent: var(--neon-cyan)=#7bf5fb, and introduces --state-ok=#54f0a0 which is a DIFFERENT green from tokens.css --success #34d399. index.css's @theme bridge (L13-34) hardcodes a THIRD copy: --color-accent:#7bf5fb, --color-success:#34d399. Net effect: the 'success/complete' green is #34d399 in the DOM-token world but #54f0a0 (poster-exact) in the superbrain world, and --accent resolves to either #3ad9e6 or #7bf5fb depending on scope. This is the duplicated/conflicting token definition the audit asks for.
locator: index.css @theme L13-34 vs tokens.css L36-55 vs superbrain.css L19-62
proposedFix: Pick the poster-exact values as canonical everywhere: success/complete green -> #54f0a0 (poster) in BOTH tokens.css --success and index.css --color-success; make tokens.css --accent resolve to #7bf5fb (the poster cyan that --accent-400 and superbrain already use) instead of the dimmer #3ad9e6 (--accent-500); document that superbrain.css --neon-* is the canonical tetrad and have the other two layers reference the same four hex values. Hue/value alignment only; no intensity change.

verdict.reason:
CONFIRMED by reading all three cited files. The conflicting token values are real: (1) --text-1 is #f2f3f7 in tokens.css L27 but rgba(232,238,250,0.93) in superbrain.css L23; (2) --hairline is 0.08 in tokens.css L23 vs 0.09 in superbrain.css L26 — and the tokens.css comment "(matches superbrain.css)" is factually FALSE; (3) --success=#34d399 (tokens.css L48) vs --state-ok/--neon-green=#54f0a0 (superbrain.css L31/L35) are different greens, and I verified against the poster legend that #54f0a0 is the poster-exact 'Complete' green; (4) --accent resolves to #3ad9e6 (tokens.css L41→accent-500 L38) vs #7bf5fb (superbrain.css L34→neon-cyan L30); (5) index.css @theme L27/L28 hardcodes a third copy (--color-accent:#7bf5fb, --color-success:#34d399). So the duplicated/conflicting SSOT defect is genuine and aligns with prior audit observations (2986/2991).

TWO CORRECTIONS to the finding. (a) MISREAD of the mechanism: the detail claims superbrain.css is 'loaded via index.css chain' and the winner 'depends on import/cascade order.' FALSE — grep shows index.css imports ONLY tokens.css (L6); superbrain.css is imported separately in SuperbrainApp.jsx L16. They both write :root, so superbrain's values win at the document root when the being mounts; the cascade-order framing is inaccurate. (b) Real-world visual impact is LOWER than implied: the actual official chrome (GagosChrome.css) hardcodes the poster hexes directly (#7bf5fb, #b06eff, #54f0a0) and consumes NONE of --accent/--success/--text-1, so the conflict mostly lives in latent/unconsumed tokens, not visible pixels. The misleading --hairline comment and duplicated SSOT remain legitimate maintenance hazards — medium holds.

RECLASSIFIED objective-safe -> needs-decision. The proposedFix BUNDLES two different kinds of change. The --success -> #54f0a0 part is a clean wrong-mapping fix against the verified poster legend (that sub-part alone is objective-safe). But the fix ALSO says make --accent '#7bf5fb instead of the dimmer #3ad9e6' — both are valid non-blue cyan-ramp values (accent-400 vs accent-500), so choosing the brighter one is a brightness/intensity change, which SACRED PALETTE LAW explicitly reserves as the operator's RTX call (never objective-safe). It also requires a product choice of WHICH layer is the canonical SSOT (tokens.css ramp vs superbrain neon tetrad). Bundled cleanup + brightness pick + SSOT choice = needs-decision, not objective-safe.

title: tokens.css --accent (=--accent-500 #3ad9e6) is an off-poster teal-cyan; the poster cyan is #7bf5fb (--accent-400)
detail: The accent ramp anchors correctly on #7bf5fb at --accent-400 (L37) but then defines --accent: var(--accent-500) (L41) = #3ad9e6, a darker, greener teal-cyan that is NOT the poster hero cyan. --accent-hover is the correct #7bf5fb. So the DEFAULT accent surface (anything using var(--accent)) renders a teal that drifts off the poster's signature cyan while hover snaps to the right hue. superbrain.css by contrast sets --accent: var(--neon-cyan) = #7bf5fb, so the two systems disagree on what 'accent' looks like at rest.
proposedFix: Point --accent at --accent-400 (#7bf5fb) so the resting accent matches the poster cyan and the superbrain layer, OR keep --accent-500 as a 'pressed/active' step but ensure the primary --accent token resolves to the poster cyan. Pure hue/value reconciliation.

title: GagosChrome send button gradient dips into #6a1eff (electric indigo) below the poster purple
detail: The send button (L333) is `linear-gradient(150deg, #b06eff, #6a1eff)`. The top stop #b06eff is the exact poster purple (good), but the bottom stop #6a1eff is a saturated electric indigo/blue-violet that sits OFF the poster purple toward blue. The poster's purple channel is #b06eff with no blue-indigo companion. This is the only chrome element that drifts off-tetrad toward indigo; the mic listening state #ff5a78/#ff9bb0 (L322-325) is a warm red accent consistent with the error/warm-red allowance, not a deviation.
proposedFix: Replace the #6a1eff bottom stop with a darker tint of the poster purple itself (e.g. color-mix toward a deeper #8a4ee6 / #7a3ad9) so the gradient stays on the cyan->purple->green->orange axis without dipping into indigo. Hue correction; gradient depth/intensity unchanged.

title: Scene MODE_TINT / uModeTint base is deep indigo-blue (#10164a, #241145) — an off-tetrad blue substrate under the brain
detail: SuperbrainScene defines uModeTint default #10164a (L207) and MODE tints observe #10164a, synthesize #35205f, orchestrate #5c183d (L267-269), plus ambientLight #241145 (L1642). #10164a and #241145 are deep INDIGO-blue, not the poster void color. superbrain.css fixed the DOM void to #030108 (deep purple-black, explicitly 'to match poster void') and the atmosphere nebula to violet rgba(80,18,140) + cyan — so the 3D scene's indigo-blue mode-tint substrate is inconsistent with the chrome's poster-purple void. These are very dark so the visible leak is small, hence low severity, but the hue family (indigo-blue) is the banned one.
proposedFix: Shift the dark mode-tint/ambient substrate from indigo-blue (#10164a, #241145) toward the poster's purple-black void family (e.g. #1a0a3a / #1c0a2e), matching superbrain.css --bg #030108 and the violet nebula. Darkness/intensity preserved; hue moved off blue. Confirm at :5173 as the brain glows over it (mild fidelity overlap).

title: Scene light rig is built from off-tetrad blues/indigos that contradict the zero-blue poster
detail: The scene <directionalLight>/<pointLight> rig (lines 1643-1648) and ambient (1642) use #8fa8ff, #bcd0ff (cornflower blues), #795cff/#5e8dff (indigo-blue), #c8a8ff (lavender) and #ff5c9a (pink). The poster brief is explicit: 'The poster has ZERO blue.' These are pure periwinkle/cornflower blues, not the sacred tetrad (cyan #7bf5fb, purple #b06eff, green #54f0a0, orange #ff7e40). In points-mode the cortex is self-lit and the comment at 1639-1640 says these lights only serve 'OTHER scene objects (accretion core)', but the rig is NOT gated by BEING_MODE the way KnowledgeHorizon/MemoryGalaxy/CognitiveGrasp are (each guarded by BEING_MODE !== 'points' at 1617/1627/1632), so any lit object (AccretionCore, materialized slabs' lit components) picks up a blue/indigo cast that reads as a hue family the poster never uses. Composition clash: introduces a cool BLUE family alongside the tetrad's cyan, muddying the 'one coherent world' read. The pink #ff5c9a is also an off-tetrad accent (poster error is warm-red #ff5c48, not magenta-pink).
proposedFix: Re-letter the light colors onto the tetrad's own cool side: replace the cornflower blues #8fa8ff/#bcd0ff/#5e8dff with cyan-leaning neutrals derived from #7bf5fb (e.g. a desaturated cyan-white), the indigo #795cff/lavender #c8a8ff with the poster purple #b06eff family, and the pink #ff5c9a with the orange/warm-red family. Keep intensities exactly as-is (intensity is the operator's RTX call); only the HUE letter changes. Alternatively gate the whole rig behind BEING_MODE !== 'points' since the points cortex is self-lit, removing the blue cast from the default scene entirely.

verdict.reason: VERIFIED by reading SuperbrainScene.tsx:1639-1648. Both factual pillars hold. (1) The light rig uses genuinely off-tetrad hues: ambient #241145 (deep indigo), directionals #8fa8ff / #bcd0ff (cornflower/periwinkle blue), #795cff (indigo), pointLights #5e8dff (cornflower blue), #c8a8ff (lavender), #ff5c9a (magenta-pink). None are the sacred tetrad (cyan #7bf5fb, purple #b06eff, green #54f0a0, orange #ff7e40), and the poster brief is explicit that the world has ZERO blue. The pink #ff5c9a is also off-tetrad (the warm accent is #ff7e40 / warm-red, not magenta). So this is a true source-level palette-law deviation. (2) The gating asymmetry is real: KnowledgeHorizon (1617), CognitiveGrasp (1627), MemoryGalaxy (1632), AccretionCore (1653-1655), PointerBrainClone (1658), NervousSystem (1666) are ALL guarded by BEING_MODE !== 'points', but the ambient/directional/point light rig (1642-1648) is NOT. readBeingMode defaults to 'points' (beingMode.ts:9 + tests: clean root with no params boots points), so this is the live default. The comment at 1639-1640 states the rig exists only for AccretionCore — yet AccretionCore is itself gated off in points mode, making the rig's stated purpose entirely inert in the default scene while still mounting. HOWEVER, the finding's IMPACT claim ('muddies the one coherent world') is overstated: the only lit (meshStandardMaterial) surfaces that survive in points mode are in MaterializedTab.tsx — the slab body (1834) whose albedo theme.body=#02070d and outline #010409 are near-BLACK (organMaterialState.ts:84-86), so the blue diffuse contribution (albedo x lightColor x N·L) is at most ~5% and effectively invisible; and the tab dots (305) + umbilical tube (1679, emissiveIntensity 1.02) which are color==emissive tetrad-derived and dominated by their own emissive + bloom. So the rendered blue cast is negligible-to-faint, not a strong clash. Net: the finding is a genuine, currently-true source-hygiene/palette violation, but low rendered severity. The proposedFix's ALTERNATIVE (gate the whole rig behind BEING_MODE !== 'points') is the correct objective-safe action: in the default points mode the rig lights nothing it was built for (AccretionCore gated off), so it is effectively dead and can only leak off-palette hue onto the few lit slab surfaces; gating it off removes the risk and preserves intensities exactly. NOTE for the fixer: a real change must also patch the lab mirror GAG demo/gag-orchestrator/src/components/canvas/SuperbrainScene.tsx (confirmed to carry the byte-identical rig at the same lines).

title: HorizonGlow default color #3a1f7a is a blue-violet, off the poster purple family
detail: HorizonGlow defaults color='#3a1f7a' (line 32), a deep blue-violet/indigo. The poster's deep core bloom reads as a magenta-violet (#b06eff family) over a near-black void, not an indigo. While HorizonGlow is gated OFF in points mode (SuperbrainScene gates KnowledgeHorizon at 1617 and the points branch comment at 1591 removes the atmosphere layer), this component is still mounted in mesh/layered mode and its hue leans toward the same off-tetrad blue-violet family as the constants/light-rig issues, so it would re-introduce the blue cast wherever it renders. Its sibling atmosphere in superbrain.css (atmosphere-layer, line 145) already uses rgba(80,18,140) which is a warmer violet — so the two atmosphere sources disagree on the purple hue family.
proposedFix: Shift the HorizonGlow default toward the poster purple family (e.g. a desaturated #b06eff-derived deep violet) so it matches the superbrain.css atmosphere-layer nebula (rgba(80,18,140)) and the REGION occipital/cerebellum violets, giving one consistent purple across the void. Hue-only change; opacity 0.16 is the RTX intensity call and stays.

title: NeuralAura spark mode colors use off-tetrad cyan/violet variants
detail: AURA_MODE_COLORS (lines 54-58) uses observe #40e8ff (a brighter electric cyan than tetrad #7bf5fb), synthesize #aef2ff (pale cyan-white, fine as a near-white), orchestrate #8f7bff (a blue-violet, cooler than poster purple #b06eff). #40e8ff and #8f7bff are close cousins but drift off the exact tetrad — #8f7bff in particular leans blue/periwinkle, the family the poster avoids. The hold amber #ffb454 is consistent with the HUD approval amber, good. The nucleus rose hue vec3(1.0,0.25,0.45) (line 133) is the brain's SSS rose and is consistent with the being's warm frontal lobe, so that is poster-faithful.
proposedFix: Nudge observe #40e8ff -> #7bf5fb (exact tetrad cyan) and orchestrate #8f7bff -> #b06eff (exact tetrad purple) so the aura sparks read in the same two dominant nerve hues as the HUD and brain crown. synthesize #aef2ff can stay as a near-white processing tint. Brightness/sat unchanged; hue letter only.

title: work-role amber-on-cyan and waiting-role periwinkle drift slightly off the tetrad but read coherent
detail: ROLE_PALETTE (lines 91-143) is well-composed against the poster: decision=orange (#ffc36e/#ff9c62) matches phase-4/approval orange, scar=warm-red (#ff7a90/#ff5f7a) matches error, memory=green (#a9fff3/#8dffd1) matches complete-green, intake=cyan (#6ef0ff/#9af7ff). Two minor drifts: (1) work role pairs reach cyan #79ebff with live AMBER #ffbe78 — a cyan+orange pairing that IS on the tetrad (both hero hues) and reads as 'working/showing-work' per the poster, so this is coherent, not a clash. (2) waiting role uses live #b9b6ff and point #bdd8ff — these are periwinkle/lavender-blue, the one family leaning toward the poster's forbidden blue rather than the warmer purple #b06eff. Across scenes the waiting tabs would carry a faint blue cast next to the purple-tetrad focus tab. Muddy-combination risk is low because these are low-saturation, but the hue family is inconsistent with the rest of the purple usage (#b06eff everywhere else).
proposedFix: Shift waiting live #b9b6ff and point #bdd8ff off the periwinkle-blue toward the tetrad purple #b06eff family (e.g. #b9a6ff / a touch warmer) so dormant tabs read as 'purple, dimmed' rather than 'blue'. Leave work/decision/scar/memory/intake as-is; they are tetrad-faithful. Hue-only; the opacity/intensity tissue scales are the RTX call.

title: Three different near-black void values across scenes (#000000 vs #030108 vs #050510 vs #050507)
detail: The cosmos void is defined in at least four places with different hex: SuperbrainScene sets <color attach='background' args={['#000000']} /> (line 1641, pure black); superbrain.css uses #030108 (poster purple-black, lines 21/125); constants.ts voidBg is #050510 (a blue-black, line 37); tokens.css --canvas is #050507 (neutral near-black, line 11). The poster void is a deep purple-black. Pure #000000 behind the canvas vs the #030108 purple-black DOM behind the chrome means the 3D layer sits on a slightly different black than the 2D layer; #050510 is faintly blue. Not a strong clash (all near-black) but it is an inconsistent base hue family across the composited scenes.
proposedFix: Pick the poster purple-black #030108 as the single canonical void and use it for the canvas background, tokens --canvas, and constants voidBg (the canvas <color> can stay near-black but should match the DOM's #030108 to avoid a seam). Removes the faint blue of #050510. Pure-black canvas is defensible if intentional, but align constants/tokens to one purple-black value.

area: HUD console glow tetrad vs brain crown hue order
title: HUD chrome correctly uses the full tetrad and is internally coherent with the brain region palette
detail: Positive/confirming finding: the HUD (superbrain.css) is the most poster-faithful layer. The console glows (lines 660-666), command-bar glow (1327-1333) and powerSurge keyframes (494-499) all cycle the exact tetrad cyan #7bf5fb -> purple #b06eff -> green #54f0a0 -> orange #ff7e40, matching the 'STATUS FROM BODY' legend. Status pills (372-414), per-phase accents (1927-1933) and GagosChrome dots (model=purple #b06eff, supervised=green #54f0a0, offline=#f87171) all map onto the tetrad/semantic set consistently. The offline #f87171 is a soft coral-red (from tokens --danger) rather than the poster warm-red #ff5c48/#ff7e40 — a minor cross-scene inconsistency since the 3D error posture uses #ff5c48 (bodyPosture) while the DOM offline uses #f87171. Otherwise the chrome, HUD, and brain region palette form one coherent tetrad world.
proposedFix: Align the one outlier: GagosChrome offline dot #f87171 and superbrain.css tamper #ff5c5c -> the poster warm-red used by bodyPosture error (#ff5c48 / rgb 255,92,72) so 'error/offline' reads as ONE red across the 3D body and the 2D chrome. No other HUD change needed; this layer is the reference for the others.

title: The 'arrival' organism phase is a DEAD branch in the live product — every runtime caller omits lifecycleState
detail: livingOrchestrator.ts:80-138 only emits phase='arrival' when input.lifecycleState==='arriving' (line 136). But every NON-test caller in the running app omits lifecycleState, so it defaults to 'rest' (line 81): MaterializationLayer.tsx:383 `deriveLivingOrchestration({ tabs, focusId, attention })`, SuperbrainScene.tsx:696 and :970, brainAttentionPosture.ts:55. lifecycleState is only ever passed in *.test.ts files. Consequence: organismLifecycle.phaseFor (organismLifecycle.ts:167-168) can never reach its `arrival` branch in product, so getOrganismPhase() is never 'arrival'. The entire arrival path that bodyPosture/pointFieldLifecycle key off (phase==='arrival') is unreachable. The Arrival cinematic only survives because BootSequence drives a SEPARATE state machine (lifecycleStateMachine) that feeds the scene's uArrival/uIgnite uniforms directly — the two FSMs are not bridged.
proposedFix: Bridge the lifecycleStateMachine snapshot into the orchestration: have MaterializationLayer (and the two SuperbrainScene useMemos) read getLifecycleSnapshot().state, map ARRIVING→'arriving' / ATTENTIVE→'attentive' / etc., and pass it as lifecycleState to deriveLivingOrchestration. Then organismPhaseBus will publish 'arrival' during the opening and the posture/lifecycle tables below will actually fire. Mirror to GAG demo/gag-orchestrator/src/lib/livingOrchestrator.ts.

verdict.reason: CONFIRMED by reading the files. livingOrchestrator.ts:81 defaults `lifecycleState` to 'rest'; the 'arrival' phase is only emitted when lifecycleState==='arriving' (line 136). Grep confirms EVERY non-test caller omits lifecycleState: MaterializationLayer.tsx:383, SuperbrainScene.tsx:696 & :970, and brainAttentionPosture.ts:55 all call `deriveLivingOrchestration({ tabs, focusId, attention })` with no lifecycleState; tabStore.ts:157/176/183 and the __getLivingOrchestration host hook likewise pass only getTabStoreSnapshot(). lifecycleState is passed ONLY in livingOrchestrator.test.ts (lines 39,52). Therefore organismLifecycle.phaseFor line 168 (`if (orchestration.phase === 'arrival')`) is unreachable in product, so getOrganismPhase() is never 'arrival' and bodyPosture's arrival→'think' mapping (bodyPosture.ts:50) never fires for the opening. The claim that the Arrival cinematic survives via a SEPARATE FSM is also confirmed: BootSequence.tsx:227 calls transitionToArriving() on lifecycleStateMachine; SuperbrainScene.tsx:1202 subscribeLifecycle and :1410-1431 drive uArrival/uIgnite directly from LifecycleState.ARRIVING — independent of deriveLivingOrchestration. The two FSMs are genuinely unbridged. This is an objective dead-branch / unplumbed-state defect, not taste. CORRECTION: severity should be MEDIUM not high — the arrival animation IS fully visible to the user (driven by the working separate FSM); the only loss is that the spectral posture-color/flow layer doesn't carry an arrival-specific tint during the ~1-2s opening, and the unreached posture (arrival→'think' purple) is hue-adjacent to the 'rest' lavender it falls back to. It is latent-correctness/dead-code, not a user-facing breakage. fixClass objective-safe is correct (bridging an FSM snapshot into an existing input field; no aesthetic/RTX judgment), though the proposed fix touches the same lab mirror noted in repo conventions.

title: During Arrival the body wears rest-lavender, not the arrival→think purple the posture map specifies
detail: bodyPosture.ts PHASE_TO_POSTURE maps arrival→'think' (purple #b06eff, flow 0.55, tint 0.46) — the 'first ignition of awareness' the poster Arrival panel shows as an igniting violet/multicolour being. But because the 'arrival' phase is never published (finding above), the scene-root posture block (SuperbrainScene.tsx:1493-1497) reads livePhase = conversationToOrganismPhase(getConversationPhase()) ?? getOrganismPhase() = 'rest' for the entire 3.5s opening, so uPosture damps to rest lavender [158,120,245] with tint 0.0. The cortex-weighted uIgnite flash (pointFieldMaterial FRAGMENT line 110, luminance-only) still fires, but the body's HUE is the wrong posture for the panel — there is no 'waking/igniting' color beat, the being just brightens in resting lavender.
proposedFix: Once the arrival phase is bridged (finding 1), getOrganismPhase() will return 'arrival' and the existing PHASE_TO_POSTURE arrival→think purple will drive uPosture automatically — no color-table change. Verify the damp (delta*3.0, line 1497) eases lavender→purple→back over the 3.5s without a jarring snap. Intensity of that purple is the operator's RTX call.

verdict.reason: CONFIRMED by reading the full chain. During the ~3.5s opening the being's posture HUE is rest-lavender, not the arrival->think purple the poster's panel-1 "first ignition of awareness" calls for. Root cause verified in product code: MaterializationLayer.tsx:383 calls deriveLivingOrchestration({ tabs, focusId, attention }) WITHOUT passing lifecycleState, so livingOrchestrator.ts:81 defaults lifecycleState='rest' and the 'arrival' branch (livingOrchestrator.ts:136) is dead in production (only the test file passes lifecycleState). The lifecycleStateMachine singleton IS set to ARRIVING at boot (BootSequence.tsx:227 / WorkspaceCanvas.tsx:136), but that signal never reaches the orchestrator, so with no tabs orchestration.phase='rest', organismLifecycle.phaseFor returns 'rest', and setOrganismPhase('rest') is published (MaterializationLayer.tsx:393). Independently, the conversation bus stays 'idle' through arrival (setConversationPhase is only called by GagosChrome on real chat turns, never at boot), so at SuperbrainScene.tsx:1493 livePhase = conversationToOrganismPhase(idle)=null ?? getOrganismPhase()='rest'. deriveBodyPosture({phase:'rest'}) = lavender [158,120,245], tint 0.0 (bodyPosture.ts:36); uPosture damps to lavender and uPostureTint to 0 (lines 1495-1507). PHASE_TO_POSTURE.arrival->'think' purple #b06eff (bodyPosture.ts:48 maps arrival, BODY_POSTURES.think = [176,110,255]) is correct and would produce the waking beat IF arrival were published — so this is purely a wiring gap, not a color-table problem. fixClass=objective-safe is correct: the fix is to thread the already-supported, already-tested lifecycleState param into the one product call site (subscribe to the lifecycle bus), no new hue/intensity invented; the purple already lives in the sacred map. The finding's own carve-out ("intensity of that purple is the operator's RTX call") correctly isolates the aesthetic part. Severity high is fair: Arrival is the poster's lead panel and "STATUS FROM BODY" (status read via hue) is a core law, though it is a subtle transient beat. Note: fix is gated on the companion 'arrival-never-published' finding being bridged, which the proposedFix honestly states.

title: Poster's 'knowledge-field streams inward' has no dedicated inflow layer in the official points frontend
detail: The Arrival panel's defining gesture is an external knowledge field (glyphs/streams) converging FROM the cosmos INTO the being ('knowledge-field streams inward / particles converge'). Two components implement exactly this — CosmicBackground's Starfield gravitational funnel (graspRange mix 25→42 on uArrival, glyphs snapping to core, CosmicBackground.tsx:191-199) and AccretionCore's inflow (uArrival alpha boost, AccretionCore.tsx:96) — but BOTH are gated to mesh mode only: AccretionCore at SuperbrainScene.tsx:1653 (`BEING_MODE !== 'points'`) and the Starfield's arrival funnel is fed arrivalScalarRef which IS passed to CosmicBackground in points mode (line 1620), HOWEVER KnowledgeHorizon (the photographic field) is also gated off (line 1617). Net: in points mode the only 'inrush' is the brain's OWN points expanding from a LOCAL per-point scatter origin (pointFieldMaterial VERTEX line 50: `origin = p + aScatter*2.0`, a 2-unit local offset) — they do NOT stream in from the wide cosmos. The CosmicBackground glyph funnel does still run in points mode and is the closest match, but it reads as background drift-by stars, not a directed field condensing into the silhouette.
proposedFix: Decide whether the points-being's Arrival should show an external field converging. Objective part: confirm CosmicBackground's uArrival funnel is actually visible/strong enough in points mode during the opening (it receives arrivalScalarRef, but arrivalScalarRef is forced to 0 for AWAKENING mode — SuperbrainScene.tsx:1435-1438 — so only true first-load COALESCENCE funnels). Aesthetic part: whether to add a points-mode knowledge-field inflow layer (density/glyph count/grasp strength) is an RTX/feel call.
locator: SuperbrainScene.tsx:1617-1655 (gating), 1435-1438 (awakening zeros funnel); CosmicBackground.tsx:191-199; AccretionCore.tsx:96; pointFieldMaterial.ts:50

verdict.reason: Verified every load-bearing claim by reading the cited files. Poster panel 1 (Arrival) names "Knowledge-field streams inward / Particles converge" as the defining gesture — confirmed against demoplan.png. (1) AccretionCore's inflow (uArrival alpha boost, AccretionCore.tsx:96) is gated to mesh only — SuperbrainScene.tsx:1653 `{BEING_MODE !== 'points' && (<AccretionCore.../>)}` — so it never runs in the official points frontend. (2) KnowledgeHorizon (photographic field) is also gated off at line 1617. (3) The point-being's OWN inrush is a LOCAL per-point scatter, not a cosmos-wide convergence: pointFieldMaterial.ts:50 `vec3 origin = p + aScatter*2.0` (a 2-unit local offset). (4) CosmicBackground IS un-gated in points mode (line 1620, no BEING_MODE check) and its funnel is real (graspRange mix 25->42, snap to vec3(0.0), CosmicBackground.tsx:191-199) — so an inward gesture DOES exist, which the finding correctly concedes. (5) Confirmed the AWAKENING zeroing: SuperbrainScene.tsx:1435-1438 sets arrivalScalarRef=0 for ARRIVING+AWAKENING, so only true first-load COALESCENCE (line 1423 arrivalTarget=env.arrival) feeds the funnel; every return-from-rest reverts CosmicBackground to the canon 25->2 grasp. Net: in points mode there is no DEDICATED being-fed knowledge-field inflow layer; the only convergence is background drift-by glyphs (first load only) and the brain's local scatter. This is a real divergence from a defining poster gesture, not a false positive or misread.

title: Point-cloud inrush is a tiny 2-unit local scatter, not a coalescence from the cosmos
detail: The poster shows particles converging across a large void to FORM the being. In code the point inrush origin is `vec3 origin = p + aScatter * 2.0` (pointFieldMaterial.ts:50) where aScatter is a unit random dir — so each point starts only ~2 world-units from its final spot and eases in over ta (line 51-52). At the points-mode camera (z=15, fov 26, SuperbrainScene.tsx:1594) a 2-unit pre-spread reads as a brief local fuzz/bloom, not a field gathering from the deep field. Additionally BrainPointField.tsx:216 caps the inrush: `arrivalTarget = Math.min(t.arrival, 1 - uniforms.uArrival.value)` and t.arrival for the 'arrival' phase is only 0.5 (pointFieldLifecycle.ts:23) — but since the 'arrival' phase is dead (finding 1), at boot the phase is 'rest' (t.arrival=1) so the cap is purely 1-uArrival from the scene envelope. The motion is correct in direction but small in amplitude vs the poster's wide convergence.
proposedFix: Amplitude of the scatter pre-spread (the `* 2.0` multiplier) and the per-point stagger band (the 0.4 in line 51) are aesthetic-rtx feel dials for how 'cosmic' the coalescence reads — flag, do not hardcode a new value. The objective sub-issue is that pointFieldLifecycle's arrival row (grow:0.5, arrival:1) is never consumed because the phase never fires; fixing finding 1 makes this row live (and then grow:0.5 during arrival is worth a deliberate look — a half-grown being during coalescence may or may not be intended).

verdict.reason: VERIFIED against the cited files. pointFieldMaterial.ts:50 does read `vec3 origin = p + aScatter * 2.0`, and aScatter is declared (line 25) as a unit scatter dir, so each point's inrush origin is only ~2 world-units from its settled position; line 51 staggers by `aBirth * 0.4` and line 52 eases in. Camera is confirmed at position [0,-0.5,15], fov 26 (SuperbrainScene.tsx:1594). title: Points-mode arrival uses a static orbit camera — no cinematic settle/pull that sells 'coalescence'

title: Only the first-ever load plays Arrival/coalescence; every return plays AWAKENING with the inflow zeroed

detail: Poster panel 2 places a labeled intake ripple at the base (the concentric INTAKE_RING_COUNT rings, comment 'poster phase 2 — you speak here') as the focal point of FIRST CONTACT: it is where the user's words enter. verdict.reason:

I computed the rings' vAxis exactly: ringBaseY = SEGMENT_BOTTOM_Y(-2.85) - 0.5 = -3.35; aBand = clamp((-3.35 - BODY_AXIS_MIN(-2.85))/(BODY_AXIS_MAX(0.7)-BODY_AXIS_MIN(-2.85)),0,1) = clamp(-0.5/3.55,0,1) = 0. (1) conversationPhaseBus.ts:19 declares 'awakening' in the ConversationPhase union; (2) conversationToOrganismPhase maps both 'awakening' and 'thinking' -> 'attentive' (lines 67-69), so they are visually identical (purple); (3) GagosChrome.jsx:42 defines awakening:{color:'#b69cff'} in PHASE_META; (4) the sole submit handler calls setConversationPhase('thinking') immediately on submit (GagosChrome.jsx:300). title: Poster's 'User attention' directional orient (being looks AT the user) is only a 1-2 degree pointer lean, not a state-driven orient toward the conversation

(1) In the shipped DEFAULT substrate (BEING_MODE==='points'; readBeingMode default + project brief both say points is official), the brain holds a STATIC pose: lines 826-832 do groupRef.rotation.set(FORWARD_LEAN, -0.78, 0) and position.set(...) with NO pointer/awaken orient. (2) The only 'looks at you' code is the CURSOR_ATTENTION block at 862-872, and it lives entirely inside the mesh-only `else` branch that opens at line 833 — so it never runs in points mode. Confirmed: attendRef tracks state.pointer.x/y (mouse), leans rotation.y by attend.x*0.035 (~2 deg) and rotation.x by -attend.y*0.022 (~1.3 deg), scaled by awakenLean=1+uAwaken*0.6. - livingWorkspaceLayout.ts:158-163 — CORNERS array exactly = [-1.32,0.62,0.25],[1.32,0.62,0.25],[-1.32,-1.02,0.25],[1.32,-1.02,0.25] (TL/TR/BL/BR), consumed for waiting tabs when input.points (line 154). Matches finding.

- MaterializedTab.tsx:1355-1378 — HUD branch positions the tab in CAMERA space: _hudPos = camera.position + camFwd*HUD_DIST + camRight*(targetLocal.x*HUD_SPREAD_X) + camUp*(targetLocal.y*HUD_SPREAD_Y); comment explicitly says 'hold a fixed SCREEN position ... independent of the being's rotation + the slow orbit'; slab lookAt(camera). So the tab BODY is screen-locked.
- Lines 1393-1401 — the NERVE tail is correctly re-rooted to a spine vertebra (NERVE_VERTEBRA_UPPER for index<=1, else LOWER), scaled by dock scale. So only the nerve touches the spine; the slab body floats at a screen corner. This exactly matches the finding's 'nerve to vertebra, but bodies at corners' description.
